comments:
Summary
This thesis introduces Function-Described Graphs (FDGs), a prototype representation for classes of attributed graphs that augments first-order (marginal) probabilistic models with qualitative second-order Boolean relations (antagonism, occurrence, existence) between pairs of vertices and arcs. The work develops: formal definitions of FDGs; methods to synthesise FDGs from sets of attributed graphs (supervised and unsupervised clustering); two Bayesian-motivated distance measures for matching attributed graphs (AGs) to FDGs (one using hard second-order constraints, one relaxing them as costs); an A* / branch-and-bound algorithm to compute optimal matchings; several efficient suboptimal matching schemes (probabilistic relaxation, expanded-vertex matching, a non-iterative filter); and clustering algorithms (incremental and hierarchical) that use FDGs. Extensive experiments are reported on random graphs, a synthetic 3D-object dataset and a small real-image office-object dataset.
Strengths
- Comprehensive: the thesis develops a complete pipeline from representation and synthesis to matching and clustering, with algorithmic details and experiments. The archival breadth (definitions, algorithms, complexity, evaluation) is impressive.  
- Conceptually clear advance: FDGs strike a useful balance between expressive joint models (intractable) and fully factorised FORGs (over-generalising) by keeping marginal distributions and adding qualitative second-order relations to curb structural over-generalisation.  
- Two matching formulations: the paper gives both a strict constraint-based matching and a softened-cost formulation, which is a pragmatic and well-motivated design for handling noisy data.  
- Practical efficiency work: branch-and-bound pruning, expanded-vertex distances and relaxation-based filters are sensible and useful engineering to make matching tractable.  
- Good experimental variety: random-graph studies clarify behaviour, and application to 3D-object views and real segmented images demonstrates applicability.
Weaknesses and limitations
- Approximation & modelling trade-offs: the FDG arc model discards conditional arc probabilities (arc attribute dependence on endpoint attributes) and replaces detailed joint statistics by coarse Boolean relations. While this is explicitly acknowledged, the thesis does not fully quantify the representational loss or its effect when conditional dependencies are important.  
- Coarseness of second-order information: antagonism/occurrence/existence are qualitative proxies for joint probabilities; they can be effective but may be too coarse in some domains. Exploring or estimating more nuanced second-order probabilities would strengthen the approach.  
- Empirical comparisons are limited: experiments compare FDGs mainly against FORG-style baselines and the Sanfeliu edit-distance/nearest-neighbour. Broader comparisons to other prototype-based or graph-embedding methods (or modern alternatives) are not present, which limits claims about relative performance.  
- Scalability: although efficient sub-optimal methods are proposed, the optimal matching still has exponential worst-case complexity. Empirical results are on modest-sized graphs (typical AGs are small); larger-scale experiments would clarify scalability in practice.  
- Sensitivity to parameters and segmentation: many thresholds and weights are set heuristically (cost weights, discretisation, segmentation parameters). The impact of these choices is not exhaustively analysed. In the real-image tests, segmentation is a key failure point yet parameters and robustness receive limited investigation.  
- Dependence on labellings for synthesis: supervised synthesis requires common labelling across AGs (addressed by clustering variants), but unsupervised labelling remains challenging and can yield merged vertices representing distinct parts.
Soundness and correctness
Derivations and algorithm descriptions are generally sound. The Bayesian framing and the move from joint probability to tractable decompositions are reasonable and well explained. The relaxation of second-order constraints into additive costs is a pragmatic engineering trade-off; it is not maximum-likelihood optimal but is well justified for robustness. The theoretical limitations (approximations) are discussed. I judge correctness of the presented material to be good, subject to the approximations the author deliberately adopts.
Clarity and presentation
The thesis is well structured and thorough. Notation is sometimes dense and could be streamlined; some algorithms would benefit from more concise pseudocode and clearer statements of inputs/outputs and computational complexity. Figures and tables are numerous and helpful. Overall readability is good for a technically mature audience.
Suggestions for improvement / future work
- Quantify the trade-off between FDG Boolean relations and fuller second-order probabilistic models: where is the sweet spot in terms of storage/time vs. representational accuracy?  
- Investigate learning (estimating) soft second-order probabilities rather than pure Boolean relations, perhaps with regularisation to manage data sparsity.  
- Provide more extensive empirical benchmarks on larger, more varied datasets, and include comparisons with other graph prototype / embedding or modern graph-matching baselines.  
- Study parameter sensitivity (cost weights, discretisation levels, segmentation settings) and propose automatic selection strategies where possible.  
- Explore dimensionality/storage/time trade-offs for dense graphs (when many arc relations would be stored).  
- Consider learning from negative examples or integrating discriminative criteria into synthesis to better separate classes.
Recommendation
This is a substantial, original and well-executed piece of work on a practically important problem (prototype representation and matching of sets of attributed graphs). I recommend acceptance (or strong consideration) for oral presentation. The methods are solid and the thesis will be of interest to researchers in structural pattern recognition and graph matching. It would be stronger with broader comparative evaluation and investigation of the second-order information modelling choices.
